message:
ffs-repair/evidence: poison the ledger after any failed append

`EvidenceLedger<W: Write>::append` was careful to buffer the full
JSON-line (ending in `\n`) in memory before issuing any I/O, so a
serialization failure could not leave a torn line on disk. That is
correct as far as it goes, but a generic `W: Write` sink can still
accept a partial prefix before reporting an I/O error — e.g. a
pipe that closed mid-stream, a full disk that fit the first half
of the line. In that state the file ends at a non-newline boundary
but the ledger wrapper happily accepts a subsequent `append`,
which concatenates onto the incomplete prefix and produces a single
corrupted JSONL line that `parse_evidence_ledger` then drops.

Track a `poisoned: bool` on the ledger:

- New field on `struct EvidenceLedger<W: Write>`. `EvidenceLedger::new`
  initialises it to `false`.
- `append(&mut self, record)` and `flush(&mut self)` now check
  `self.poisoned` at entry; if set, return a synthetic I/O error
  with kind `Other` and a stable message ("evidence ledger is
  poisoned after a previous I/O failure") without touching the
  writer. Callers that want to recover can observe the poisoned
  state explicitly and take action (open a new file, rotate the
  ledger, etc.) rather than continuing to write garbage.
- On any `writer.write_all(&buf)` or `writer.flush()` failure,
  set `self.poisoned = true` **before** propagating the error, so
  the next `append` never raced past the poison check.

Doc comment on `append` rewritten to describe both invariants in
one place: the in-memory serialize-before-write buffering remains,
and the poison-on-failure invariant is spelled out so future
implementors know not to ask "why can't I retry after a write
error?". The failure-mode rationale is now "a generic [Write] can
still accept a partial prefix before reporting an I/O error; after
any write or flush failure, the ledger is poisoned so later records
cannot be concatenated onto a possibly incomplete JSONL line."

No public API rename. `EvidenceLedger::new` still returns an
`EvidenceLedger` directly, just one whose internal state includes
a poison flag. Existing `parse_evidence_ledger` callers that read
back a (possibly-poisoned) ledger file observe exactly the same
bytes they would have read before — the ledger stops writing once
poisoned, it does not rewrite or truncate prior contents.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
